request.question: Mick Jackson and Drew Renaud, have which mutual occupation?

title: Yuri Nosenko: Double Agent
Mick Jackson (director)
Mick Jackson (author)
Denial (2016 film)
Volcano (1997 film)
Chattahoochee (film)
Drew Renaud
'Igginbottom
Torrente 3: El protector
Covert One: The Hades Factor
sentences: Yuri Nosenko: Double Agent is a 1986 British television drama produced by the BBC in association with Primetime Television Ltd. and directed by Mick Jackson.
Mick Jackson (born 4 October 1943 in Grays, England, United Kingdom) is a British film director and television producer.
Between 1973 and 1987, Jackson directed many documentary and drama productions for BBC TV and Channel 4.
Relocating to Hollywood, he directed feature films, including "The Bodyguard" starring Kevin Costner and Whitney Houston.
In 2010, Jackson won an Emmy for Outstanding Directing for a Miniseries, Movie or a Dramatic Special for the dramatised biographical TV film "Temple Grandin".
Mick Jackson (born 1960) is a British writer from England, best known for his novel "The Underground Man" (1997).
The book, based on the life of William Cavendish-Scott-Bentinck, 5th Duke of Portland, was shortlisted for the Man Booker Prize and for the 1997 Whitbread Award for best first novel.
Denial is a 2016 historical drama film directed by Mick Jackson and written by David Hare, based on Deborah Lipstadt's book "History on Trial: My Day in Court with a Holocaust Denier."
It dramatises the "Irving v Penguin Books Ltd" case, in which Lipstadt, a Holocaust scholar, was sued by Holocaust denier David Irving for libel.
It stars Rachel Weisz, Tom Wilkinson, Timothy Spall, Andrew Scott, Jack Lowden, Caren Pistorius and Alex Jennings.
Volcano is a 1997 American disaster film directed by Mick Jackson and produced by Andrew Z. Davis, Neal H. Moritz and Lauren Shuler Donner.
The storyline was conceived from a screenplay written by Jerome Armstrong and Billy Ray.
The film features Tommy Lee Jones, Anne Heche, and Don Cheadle.
Jones is cast as the head of the Los Angeles County Office of Emergency Management (LAC OEM) which has complete authority in the event of an emergency or natural disaster.
His character attempts to divert the path of a dangerous lava flow through the streets of Los Angeles following the formation of a volcano at the La Brea Tar Pits.
Chattahoochee is a 1989 American drama film directed by Mick Jackson and starring Gary Oldman and Dennis Hopper.
The film is based on the real-life experiences of Chris Calhoun, who met screenwriter James Hicks, who then wrote a script based on his internment in a Florida state mental institution.

It was turned down by several major studios before being accepted by Hemdale Film Corporation, a small British-owned, Los Angeles-based company that also produced "Platoon", "Hoosiers", "The Last Emperor", and "Salvador".
Michael "Drew" Renaud (born 1985) is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer.
He has received awards from the San Diego Film Festival and LA Shorts Fest.
'Igginbottom were an English progressive rock band, featuring Steven Robinson (guitar), Allan Holdsworth (guitar, vocals), Dave Freeman (drums) and Mick Skelly (bass).
They were managed by a company called Mimo, which was owned by Mick Jackson, Mo Bacon, Ronnie Scott and his business partner Pete King.
Their only album, "'Igginbottom's Wrench", was released in 1969.
Torrente 3: El protector is a 2005 Spanish black comedy film directed, written, produced and starring Santiago Segura as the lead character, Spanish cop José Luis Torrente.
The film was the third in the popular Torrente series (following ) and was successful at the box office.
Santiago Segura decided to make a parody of the American film "The Bodyguard", which was directed by Mick Jackson and starred Kevin Costner and Whitney Houston.
Covert One: The Hades Factor (a.k.a. "Robert Ludlum's Covert One: The Hades Factor", "The Hades Factor") is a made-for-TV thriller filmed in Toronto that first aired in 2006.
Directed by Mick Jackson, the miniseries is loosely based on "The Hades Factor", a 2000 novel written by Gayle Lynds as part of the Covert-One series created by Robert Ludlum.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Mick Jackson and Drew Renaud, have which mutual occupation?
hop1_docs: Drew Renaud | Michael "Drew" Renaud (born 1985) is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer.  He has received awards from the San Diego Film Festival and LA Shorts Fest.

Mick Jackson (singer) | Michael George "Mick" Jackson (born 2 November 1947) is an English singer-songwriter and the co-writer of the song, "Blame It on the Boogie".  The song was co-authored by Mick's brother David Jackson and Elmar Krohn, and produced by Sylvester Levay.

Mick Jackson (director) | Mick Jackson (born 4 October 1943 in Grays, England, United Kingdom) is a British film director and television producer.  Between 1973 and 1987, Jackson directed many documentary and drama productions for BBC TV and Channel 4.  Relocating to Hollywood, he directed feature films, including "The Bodyguard" starring Kevin Costner and Whitney Houston.  In 2010, Jackson won an Emmy for Outstanding Directing for a Miniseries, Movie or a Dramatic Special for the dramatised biographical TV film "Temple Grandin".

Hernani Almeida | Hernani Almeida (born 7 April 1978 in Sao Vicente, Cape Verde) is a Cape Verdean musician, having started at age 7 his musical career via a small keyboard switching at the age of 14 to the electric guitar, an instrument that became his brand image.  After 1989 he joined the group's musical Mick Lima appears in 1994 with his rock band named What, which drew the attention as a guitarist of the music greats of Cape Verde.

Mick Jackson (author) | Mick Jackson (born 1960) is a British writer from England, best known for his novel "The Underground Man" (1997).  The book, based on the life of William Cavendish-Scott-Bentinck, 5th Duke of Portland, was shortlisted for the Man Booker Prize and for the 1997 Whitbread Award for best first novel.

'Igginbottom | 'Igginbottom were an English progressive rock band, featuring Steven Robinson (guitar), Allan Holdsworth (guitar, vocals), Dave Freeman (drums) and Mick Skelly (bass).  They were managed by a company called Mimo, which was owned by Mick Jackson, Mo Bacon, Ronnie Scott and his business partner Pete King.  Their only album, "'Igginbottom's Wrench", was released in 1969.

The Comforters | The Comforters is the first novel by Scottish author Muriel Spark.  She drew on experiences as a recent convert to Catholicism and having suffered hallucinations due to using Dexedrine, an amphetamine then available over the counter for dieting.  Although completed in late 1955, the book was not published until 1957.  A mutual friend, novelist Alan Barnsley, had sent the proofs to Evelyn Waugh.  At the time Waugh was writing "The Ordeal of Gilbert Pinfold," which dealt with his own drug-induced hallucinations.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Mick Jackson (multiple people) and Drew Renaud — occupational descriptors

- Drew Renaud: "Michael 'Drew' Renaud (born 1985) is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer." (passage [1]) — roles: film director; screenwriter; editor; producer; birth year 1985.
- Mick Jackson — candidates in the passages:
  - Mick Jackson (director): "is a British film director and television producer." (passage [3]) — birth date 4 October 1943.
  - Mick Jackson (singer): "is an English singer-songwriter" (passage [2]) — born 2 November 1947.
  - Mick Jackson (author): "is a British writer" (passage [5]) — born 1960.
- Explicit mutual fact supported: both Drew Renaud and Mick Jackson (director) are described as "film director" in the passages.
- Ambiguity/missing fact: which "Mick Jackson" the question intends is not specified (three distinct Mick Jacksons appear). Also ambiguous whether "producer" is the same role: Drew is a "producer" while Mick (director) is explicitly a "television producer" — label "producer" equivalence is not fully resolved by the passages.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1. Fetch "Mick Jackson (director)" page — lede/infobox and "Career"/"Filmography" or similar sections to confirm occupations (film director vs television producer) and dates.
2. Fetch "Drew Renaud" page — lede/infobox to confirm occupations and verify "producer" credit.
3. Fetch "Mick Jackson (disambiguation)" or the main "Mick Jackson" disambiguation page to confirm which Mick Jackson is intended and to cross-check occupations.
4. If needed, search keywords: "Mick Jackson film director television producer Temple Grandin" and "Drew Renaud filmography producer" for supporting sources.
response.hop2_query: Mick Jackson Drew Renaud mutual occupation film director evidence
hop2_docs: Drew Renaud | Michael "Drew" Renaud (born 1985) is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer.  He has received awards from the San Diego Film Festival and LA Shorts Fest.

Mick Jackson (director) | Mick Jackson (born 4 October 1943 in Grays, England, United Kingdom) is a British film director and television producer.  Between 1973 and 1987, Jackson directed many documentary and drama productions for BBC TV and Channel 4.  Relocating to Hollywood, he directed feature films, including "The Bodyguard" starring Kevin Costner and Whitney Houston.  In 2010, Jackson won an Emmy for Outstanding Directing for a Miniseries, Movie or a Dramatic Special for the dramatised biographical TV film "Temple Grandin".

Michael Jackson's Ghosts | Michael Jackson's Ghosts is a 1996 short film starring Michael Jackson, co-written by horror novelist Stephen King alongside Mick Garris and directed by film director and special effects guru Stan Winston which could also be classified as a long-form music video.  It was filmed and first screened in 1996 and released along with select prints of the film "Thinner".  It was released as promo a year later internationally on LaserDisc, VHS and Video CD.  Jackson plays a total of five roles in the film.

Mick Audsley | Mick Audsley (b. 1949, London, England) is a British film and television editor with more than 30 film credits.  Audsley has had a notable collaboration with the director Stephen Frears from 1982 to their latest, "Tamara Drewe".  Audsley was nominated for the BAFTA Award for Best Editing for "Dangerous Liaisons" (Frears-1988), and he won the BAFTA TV Award (Best Film or Video Editor (Fiction/Entertainment)) for "The Snapper" (Frears-1993).  Audsley has had a comparably extended collaboration with director Mike Newell, including "Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire" (2005 (Based on the Sound Editions of Tom Finan who editing Hercules)).

Mick Jackson (singer) | Michael George "Mick" Jackson (born 2 November 1947) is an English singer-songwriter and the co-writer of the song, "Blame It on the Boogie".  The song was co-authored by Mick's brother David Jackson and Elmar Krohn, and produced by Sylvester Levay.
Mick Davis (director) | Michael "Mick" Davis (born 1 August 1961) is a Scottish film director, producer and screenwriter.
Drew Pollins | Drew Pollins is an American/Canadian film director, film producer, and screenwriter.
response.summary_2: {"summary":"Explicit mutual fact supported: both Drew Renaud and Mick Jackson (director) are described as \"film director\" in the passages. Mick Jackson is a British film director and television producer (born 4 October 1943). Drew Renaud is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer (born 1985)."}
